Back-propagation is the heart of machine learning. It is what makes machines smarter.
The back-propagation is calculated after the forward pass. The forward pass can sometimes be called the “predict”.
It is intended to find and calculate how far each input’s tiny change affects the total loss, L.
Back-propagation is calculated using calculus. In calculus, the Chain Rule is used to calculate how far an input change affects the Loss. You start at the very end and work your way backward, multiplying the gradients along the way.
For this blog post, we will demonstrate how back-propagation works using a simple example.
The Forward Pass
Let’s say we have two inputs, v and w. We multiply them to get the output, h.
The output, h eventually leads to a scalar loss, L. The loss is the difference between the expected value of h and the predicted value of h.
This loss, L is what we will use to calculate the rate of change of the inputs with respect to the loss, L.
So, if we set
v = 4, and w = 2
h will be 4 * 2 = 8.
